content.text: Securing a FastAPI application involves more than adding a password check — it requires a layered approach covering identity verification, token lifecycle management, permission enforcement, and protection against protocol-level attacks.
Authentication and security in FastAPI encompasses the mechanisms for verifying
what they are allowed to do
(authorization), and protecting the API against a broad class of protocol-level and application-level attacks.
FastAPI provides first-class support for standard security schemes through its

module, tight integration with the OAuth2 and OpenID Connect specifications, and a dependency injection system that makes security logic composable, reusable, and testable. The framework builds on Starlette's middleware infrastructure for lower-level protections like CORS and HTTPS enforcement.

APIs are the attack surface of modern applications. Unlike browser-rendered web apps where the server controls the UI, APIs expose raw data and operations directly to any HTTP client. Without deliberate security design:
Unauthenticated endpoints expose private data or allow unauthorized mutations
Weak token schemes allow session hijacking or token forgery
Missing authorization checks allow privilege escalation (accessing other users' data)
Absent CORS policies allow malicious browser-based cross-origin requests
Unvalidated inputs allow injection attacks (covered partly by Pydantic but requiring additional care)
Plaintext credential transmission allows credential theft on untrusted networks
FastAPI's security primitives make the correct approach straightforward while still being flexible enough for complex real-world requirements.
Verify JWT signature, validate API key
User ID, email, roles, tenant ID
These are distinct layers. An authenticated user may not be authorized for a specific resource. Most security bugs in APIs are authorization failures, not authentication failures.
Passwords must never be stored in plaintext. FastAPI's ecosystem standard is
It is intentionally slow (configurable work factor), making brute-force attacks computationally expensive
It incorporates a random salt per hash, preventing rainbow table attacks
It is well-audited and widely deployed
MD5, SHA-1, or even SHA-256 alone for password storage — they are too fast and lack salting by design.
The most common authentication pattern for FastAPI APIs is the
Server verifies credentials, issues a signed JWT access token
Server verifies the JWT signature and extracts claims (user ID, roles, expiry)

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, List

class UserInDB(BaseModel):
    user_id: str
    username: str
    hashed_password: str
    email: str
    roles: List[str] = []
    is_active: bool = True

class UserPublic(BaseModel):
    user_id: str
    username: str
    email: str
    roles: List[str]
body — the format required by the OAuth2 specification.
The most powerful FastAPI pattern for authentication is building a reusable

from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, status
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/auth/token")

async def get_current_user(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)) -> UserInDB:
    token_data = decode_token(token)  # Raises 401 if invalid
    user = await get_user_by_id(token_data.sub)
    if user is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="User not found")
    if not user.is_active:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Inactive user")
    return user

async def get_current_active_user(
    current_user: UserInDB = Depends(get_current_user)
) -> UserInDB:
    return current_user  # Additional checks can be chained here
Use this dependency in any endpoint:
The key advantage: the authentication logic is written
and reused across any number of endpoints through injection. Updating the logic (e.g., adding token revocation checks) updates all protected endpoints simultaneously.
Authorization rules are implemented as additional dependencies that build on the authentication dependency:
For ownership checks (a user can only access their own resources):
when the resource exists but the user doesn't own it — returning
leaks the existence of the resource to unauthorized users.
Short-lived access tokens (15–30 minutes) reduce exposure if stolen, but require users to re-authenticate frequently. Refresh tokens solve this:
: short-lived (15–30 min), sent with every request
: long-lived (7–30 days), used only to obtain a new access token, stored securely (httpOnly cookie)
For server-to-server or developer API access (webhooks, SDKs, integrations):

from fastapi.security import APIKeyHeader, APIKeyQuery, APIKeyCookie
from fastapi import Security

api_key_header = APIKeyHeader(name="X-API-Key", auto_error=False)
api_key_query = APIKeyQuery(name="api_key", auto_error=False)

async def get_api_key(
    header_key: str = Security(api_key_header),
    query_key: str = Security(api_key_query),
) -> str:
    key = header_key or query_key
    if not key:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="API key required")

    # Validate against hashed keys in DB
    api_key_record = await lookup_api_key(key)
    if not api_key_record or not verify_api_key(key, api_key_record.hashed_key):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Invalid API key")

    return api_key_record

@app.get("/data/")
async def get_data(api_key: str = Depends(get_api_key)):
    return {"data": "..."}
Store a hash of the API key, not the plaintext (use bcrypt or BLAKE2)
at creation time — never store or display it again
Associate keys with scopes/permissions at the DB level
Log key usage with rate limiting per key
FastAPI itself does not terminate TLS — this is handled by the deployment layer. In production:
(nginx, Caddy) terminates TLS and forwards HTTP to Uvicorn
(AWS ALB, GCP LB) handle TLS termination
For local development or simple deployments, Uvicorn can serve HTTPS directly:
To redirect HTTP to HTTPS at the application level:
for all HTTP requests, pointing to the HTTPS equivalent.
CORS controls which browser origins can make requests to the API. Without proper CORS headers, browsers block cross-origin API calls from JavaScript — the browser enforces this, not the server.

from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://app.example.com", "https://admin.example.com"],
    allow_credentials=True,    # Required to send cookies/auth headers cross-origin
    allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "PATCH"],
    allow_headers=["Authorization", "Content-Type", "X-Request-ID"],
    expose_headers=["X-Total-Count", "X-Page"],  # Headers JS can read
    max_age=600,               # Cache preflight for 10 minutes
)

# NEVER do this in production — allows any origin
allow_origins=["*"]

# NEVER combine wildcard origins with credentials
allow_origins=["*"],
allow_credentials=True  # Browsers will block this anyway, but signals bad intent

from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from starlette.requests import Request

ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS = [r"https://.*\.example\.com$"]

import re

class DynamicCORSMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] == "http":
            headers = dict(scope.get("headers", []))
            origin = headers.get(b"origin", b"").decode()
            if any(re.match(p, origin) for p in ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS):
                # Process and add CORS headers dynamically
                pass
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
Prevents HTTP Host header injection attacks by whitelisting valid hostnames:

from fastapi.middleware.trustedhost import TrustedHostMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    TrustedHostMiddleware,
    allowed_hosts=["api.example.com", "*.example.com", "localhost"]
)
FastAPI has no built-in rate limiting — it must be added via middleware or external tooling.
For multi-process or multi-instance deployments, rate limit state must be shared via Redis:

import redis.asyncio as aioredis
from fastapi import Request, HTTPException

redis_client = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost")

async def check_rate_limit(key: str, max_requests: int, window: int):
    pipe = redis_client.pipeline()
    now = int(time.time())
    window_key = f"ratelimit:{key}:{now // window}"

    pipe.incr(window_key)
    pipe.expire(window_key, window * 2)
    results = await pipe.execute()

    request_count = results[0]
    if request_count > max_requests:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=429,
            detail="Rate limit exceeded",
            headers={
                "X-RateLimit-Limit": str(max_requests),
                "X-RateLimit-Remaining": "0",
                "Retry-After": str(window - (now % window))
            }
        )

@app.middleware("http")
async def rate_limit_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    client_ip = request.client.host
    await check_rate_limit(f"ip:{client_ip}", max_requests=100, window=60)
    return await call_next(request)
HTTP security headers protect against browser-level attacks. Add them via middleware:

from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from fastapi import Request

class SecurityHeadersMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        response = await call_next(request)
        response.headers["X-Content-Type-Options"] = "nosniff"
        response.headers["X-Frame-Options"] = "DENY"
        response.headers["X-XSS-Protection"] = "1; mode=block"
        response.headers["Strict-Transport-Security"] = "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains"
        response.headers["Referrer-Policy"] = "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
        response.headers["Content-Security-Policy"] = (
            "default-src 'self'; "
            "script-src 'self'; "
            "object-src 'none'"
        )
        response.headers["Permissions-Policy"] = "geolocation=(), camera=(), microphone=()"
        return response

app.add_middleware(SecurityHeadersMiddleware)

# WEAK: HS256 with shared secret — anyone with the secret can forge tokens
jwt.encode(payload, SECRET_KEY, algorithm="HS256")

# STRONGER: RS256 with asymmetric keys — private key signs, public key verifies
# Useful when multiple services verify tokens (they only need the public key)
from jose import jwt
jwt.encode(payload, private_key_pem, algorithm="RS256")
jwt.decode(token, public_key_pem, algorithms=["RS256"])
Always explicitly specify allowed algorithms when decoding:

# VULNERABLE: accepts any algorithm the token header specifies
jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY)

# SAFE: whitelists the expected algorithm
jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=["HS256"])
attack (where a forged token claims no signature is needed) is prevented by always specifying allowed algorithms.
Standard claims to include in tokens:
— audience (which service the token is for)
— JWT ID (unique token identifier, used for revocation)
JWTs are stateless by design — once issued, a valid token is accepted until it expires. This is a trade-off: low latency (no DB lookup per request) but no real-time revocation.
Use access tokens with very short TTL (5–15 minutes). Revocation happens implicitly at expiry. The refresh token can be revoked in the DB.
claims in Redis until their expiry:

import redis.asyncio as aioredis

redis_client = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost")

async def revoke_token(jti: str, expires_in_seconds: int):
    await redis_client.setex(f"revoked_jti:{jti}", expires_in_seconds, "1")

async def is_token_revoked(jti: str) -> bool:
    return await redis_client.exists(f"revoked_jti:{jti}") == 1

async def get_current_user(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    payload = decode_token(token)
    jti = payload.get("jti")

    if jti and await is_token_revoked(jti):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Token has been revoked")

    return await get_user_by_id(payload["sub"])
This approach adds one Redis lookup per request — typically sub-millisecond latency.
Scopes define fine-grained permissions within an OAuth2 token. A user may be authenticated but a token may only carry permissions for specific operations:

Incoming Request
        │
        ▼
[ TLS Termination ] ──── nginx / ALB / Caddy
        │
        ▼
[ TrustedHostMiddleware ] ─── Reject invalid Host headers
        │
        ▼
[ HTTPSRedirectMiddleware ] ── Force HTTPS
        │
        ▼
[ SecurityHeadersMiddleware ] ─ Add CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options
        │
        ▼
[ CORSMiddleware ] ─────────── Validate Origin header
        │
        ▼
[ RateLimitMiddleware ] ─────── Per-IP / per-key request throttling
        │
        ▼
[ Router & Endpoint ]
        │
        ▼
[ Authentication Dependency ] ─ Verify JWT / API key
        │
        ▼
[ Authorization Dependency ] ── Check roles / scopes / ownership
        │
        ▼
  Business Logic
        │
        ▼
[ Response Model Filtering ] ── Strip sensitive fields from output
        │
        ▼
HTTP Response
Each layer independently handles a category of threat. A failure or bypass at one layer is caught by the next.
Tenant isolation (users can only access their own tenant's data)
Always use environment variables or a secrets manager:

import os
SECRET_KEY = os.environ["JWT_SECRET_KEY"]  # Raises if not set — safe default
when a resource exists but the user doesn't own it confirms the resource exists to an attacker. Return
jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, options={"verify_exp": False})  # Never in production
. Disabling expiry verification means stolen tokens are permanently valid.
is accessible to any JavaScript on the page, making tokens vulnerable to XSS. Store access tokens in memory (JS variable) and refresh tokens in
is acceptable for fully public, read-only APIs. For any API handling authenticated requests, it is a critical misconfiguration.
6. Using the same secret for access and refresh tokens
If an attacker obtains the secret, they can forge both token types. Use separate secrets with different rotation schedules.
7. No rate limiting on the auth endpoint
endpoint is the primary brute-force target. Always apply strict rate limiting specifically to authentication endpoints, separate from general API limits.

# BAD — tells attacker whether username exists
detail="Password incorrect for user alice@example.com"

# GOOD — reveals nothing about which field was wrong
detail="Incorrect username or password"
(15–30 min) and use refresh token rotation for long sessions.
before storing — treat them like passwords. Use bcrypt or BLAKE2b.
Use dependency injection for all auth logic
— never repeat authentication checks inline in endpoint bodies.
to all authenticated endpoints to prevent accidental sensitive field exposure.
(login, logout, failed attempts, token refresh) with user ID, IP, and timestamp — without logging the credentials or tokens themselves.
for fine-grained OAuth2 scope enforcement rather than just role strings.
header in JWT) to support zero-downtime rotation.
via middleware for every response — CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options.
— write tests that verify unauthenticated requests are rejected, cross-tenant access is denied, and role checks are enforced.

JWT verification (signature check + claims decode) is a pure cryptographic operation — no DB or network call required. At scale, this is a significant advantage: 10,000 RPS doesn't require 10,000 DB auth lookups. The cryptographic overhead is typically sub-millisecond per token for HS256.

In microservice architectures where multiple services need to verify tokens, RS256 (asymmetric) is preferable. Each service only needs the public key, eliminating shared-secret distribution and reducing the blast radius if one service is compromised. The asymmetric operation is slower but the difference is negligible compared to typical I/O latency.
Redis for Rate Limiting and Revocation

Both token revocation (JTI blocklist) and rate limiting require shared state across workers. Redis is the standard choice — it handles millions of operations per second with sub-millisecond latency and has native support for expiring keys (perfect for token blacklists and sliding windows).
Authentication queries (user lookup on login, API key lookup) should use an async connection pool (e.g.,
pool) to avoid connection overhead per request. Size the pool based on the auth endpoint throughput, not the general API pool.
AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager
Authentication (who you are) and authorization (what you can do) are distinct layers — most API security vulnerabilities are authorization failures occurring after successful authentication.
FastAPI's dependency injection system is the correct mechanism for all auth logic — define
once, compose role/scope checkers on top, and inject them per endpoint.
JWTs are stateless and fast but not revocable without additional infrastructure (Redis JTI blocklist or short expiry + refresh rotation). Choose the trade-off explicitly.
Passwords must be hashed with bcrypt (or Argon2) — never with MD5, SHA-1, or unsalted SHA-256.
Security headers, CORS configuration, HTTPS enforcement, and rate limiting must all be applied as middleware layers — they protect the entire API surface without per-endpoint repetition.
) when an authenticated user accesses a resource they don't own —
leaks the existence of the resource.
Secrets (JWT signing keys, DB credentials, API keys) must never appear in source code — load from environment variables or a dedicated secrets manager.
endpoint requires dedicated, stricter rate limiting than the rest of the API — it is the primary brute-force target and must be treated as such.
